Title: The GluTR-binding protein is the hemebinding factor for feedback control of glutamyl-tRNA reductase

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: GluTR-binding protein; structurally homologous to bacterial heme oxygenase HugZ
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Binds heme and thereby loses affinity for GluTR1, promoting GluTR1 destabilization and feedback suppression of ALA synthesis
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Precursor for chlorophyll and heme biosynthesis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GBP is a heme-binding protein validated by hemin-agarose binding assays. 2. GBP binds GluTR1 with nanomolar affinity in vitro, but hemin reduces GBP-GluTR1 affinity by 7- to 14-fold. 3. Loss of GBP accelerates GluTR1 degradation, supporting GBP as a stabilizer of GluTR1 in the absence of heme and a mediator of heme-triggered feedback control.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: gbp-2 mutant analysis; hemin-agarose binding assay; GST pull-down; microscale thermophoresis (MST); western blot
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 1 mM ALA feeding in darkness; hemin addition in vitro
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of GBP reduces GluTR1 stability and may impair proper tetrapyrrole homeostasis
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to tune tetrapyrrole flux and balance chlorophyll/heme synthesis, but manipulation must preserve photoprotection and plastid metabolic homeostasis
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: qRT-PCR of related pathway genes; no accession reported
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts as a heme-responsive stabilizer rather than enzyme or transcription factor; proposed to mask the N-terminal Clp recognition region of GluTR1 when heme is absent.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for efficient ALA/heme-induced proteolysis of GluTR1
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Precursor for chlorophyll and heme biosynthesis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. clpc1-1 attenuates ALA-induced GluTR1 degradation at 4 h and 24 h. 2. Reduced Clp activity increases GluTR1 accumulation under standard conditions. 3. ClpC1 participates in the proteolytic arm of heme feedback regulation on ALA synthesis.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: clpc1-1 mutant; ALA feeding; western blot of GluTR1; Pchlide and heme measurement
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced Clp activity elevates GluTR1 accumulation and may broadly alter chloroplast proteostasis
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Not an obvious engineering target due to central proteostasis function and risk of pleiotropy
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as a core gene because mutant analysis directly tested causality in GluTR1/heme feedback proteolysis.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to efficient ALA/heme-induced degradation of GluTR1
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Precursor for chlorophyll and heme biosynthesis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. clpr2-1 delays ALA-induced GluTR1 proteolysis. 2. Lower Clp activity correlates with elevated GluTR1 abundance. 3. ClpR2 is part of the protease machinery implementing heme-triggered post-translational control of GluTR1.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: clpr2-1 mutant; ALA feeding; western blot of GluTR1; metabolite measurements
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Perturbing ClpR2 may broadly affect chloroplast protein turnover
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Limited due to broad plastid proteostasis role
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as a direct mechanistic component supported by mutant phenotypes.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Glutamyl-tRNA reductase; N-terminal regulatory domain/RED required for regulated proteolysis
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Common upstream ALA-forming step before chlorophyll and heme branches
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Rate-limiting enzyme and major control point for ALA synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces glutamyl-tRNA to glutamate-1-semialdehyde, the first committed intermediate toward ALA formation in plants
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool for chlorophyll and heme biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: ALA feeding triggers rapid post-translational destabilization of GluTR1; heme/GBP/Clp-dependent control lowers GluTR1 abundance and likely restricts ALA synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Universal precursor for chlorophyll and heme biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GluTR1 is specifically degraded upon ALA feeding in darkness, independent of HEMA1 transcript decline. 2. Degradation requires the N-terminal RED and is attenuated in clp mutants. 3. Heme, especially FC2-derived heme acting through GBP, triggers release of GBP from GluTR1 and enables Clp-dependent proteolysis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: ALA feeding; western blot; qRT-PCR; hema1 complementation with full-length vs ΔRED construct; clpc1-1/clpr2-1 mutants; fc2-2 mutant; chloroplast feeding with hemin; in vitro pull-down and MST
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Predominantly expressed in green tissue; transcript decreases strongly during dark incubation
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 1 mM ALA feeding; dark incubation; hemin treatment; DP and ACI inhibitor treatments
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Overstabilization may elevate tetrapyrrole intermediates and phototoxicity risk; strong degradation could limit chlorophyll biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Engineering RED-mediated stability or GBP interaction could tune tetrapyrrole flux, but requires balancing chlorophyll supply with avoidance of phototoxic intermediate overaccumulation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Truncation of the RED abolishes ALA-induced degradation, demonstrating this N-terminal region is essential for heme-responsive proteolysis.

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Downstream terminal step of heme branch
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established as rate-limiting; dominant plastid heme-producing isoform in this context
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Inserts ferrous iron into protoporphyrin IX to form heme
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms regulatory heme that triggers GluTR1 degradation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of FC2 reduces free heme accumulation and markedly stabilizes GluTR1 during ALA feeding, demonstrating FC2-derived heme is required for feedback degradation of GluTR1
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Cofactor and feedback signal regulating tetrapyrrole synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. fc2-2 plants accumulate more GluTR1 under standard conditions and retain GluTR1 during short-term ALA feeding. 2. FC2 deficiency lowers free heme and prevents ALA-induced free heme accumulation. 3. These data identify FC2-derived heme as the major signal promoting GluTR1 degradation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: fc2-2 knockout mutant; ALA feeding; western blot; qRT-PCR; measurement of ProtoIX, Pchlide, ncb heme and free heme
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: FC2 mRNA decreases in darkness according to qRT-PCR described for WT
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: GBP (functional downstream target of FC2-derived heme, not direct physical interaction shown)
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: fc2 deficiency perturbs plastid heme status and causes tetrapyrrole imbalance; linked discussion mentions flu-like/light-dependent necrosis in fc2-2
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating FC2 may strongly alter plastid heme/chlorophyll homeostasis but carries substantial risk of photodamage and chlorosis
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: qRT-PCR; metabolite profiling; no accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: FC1 knockdown showed WT-like GluTR1 stability, supporting isoform-specific contribution of FC2-derived heme to this feedback pathway.
